1: The town gate (šaʿar H8179) was the primary civic space in an ancient Israelite city — the place where elders sat to adjudicate legal disputes (Deuteronomy 21:19; Ruth 22:15; Amos 5:10-12), where commercial transactions were witnessed (Genesis 23:10-20; 2 Kings 7:1), and where prophets addressed the community (Jeremiah 17:19-20). Boaz's deliberate positioning at the gate to intercept the nearer go'el reflects sophisticated social strategy: he intends to resolve the legal matter immediately, publicly, and irrevocably, in the one venue where it will be permanently recorded in communal memory. The gate was both the town's legal courthouse and its record-keeping institution — there were no written contracts; the public witness of the elders was the legal record.
2: Boaz takes ten elders of the city as witnesses — a number that in later Jewish legal tradition (the minyan, the quorum of ten for formal prayer and legal proceedings) becomes standardized as the minimum for binding communal legal action. In Iron Age Israelite towns, the elders were the senior male heads of household who constituted the town's governing council and judicial body; their witness made a transaction legally binding and enforceable. The ten witnesses ensure that the transaction about to occur will be recognized by the entire community and cannot later be contested on grounds of insufficient witnesses.
3: Boaz frames the case around Naomi's land: 'Naomi, who has come back from the country of Moab, is selling (mākĕrāh) the piece of land that belonged to our relative Elimelech.' The sale or pledge of family land (ʾaḥuzzāh H272) was the legal trigger for the go'el's right of redemption (Leviticus 25:25: 'If your brother becomes poor and sells part of his property, then his nearest redeemer shall come and redeem what his brother has sold'). Whether Naomi is actually selling the land or offering it for redemption is debated; the Hebrew can mean either an already-completed sale (she sold it during her absence in Moab) or a prospective sale. The legal mechanism is the same either way: the nearer kinsman has first right of redemption.
4: Boaz's formal presentation of the redemption right — 'I thought I would tell you of it and say, Buy it in the presence of those sitting here and in the presence of the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem it. But if you will not, tell me, that I may know, for there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I come after you' — is a precise legal formula. The sequence mirrors the formal legal procedure: identify the right, present it to the nearest kinsman first, establish witness, and create a public record of acceptance or refusal. The nearer go'el's initial answer ('I will redeem it') is legally normal — land redemption was attractive, preserving the family patrimony and adding to one's own holdings.
5: Boaz's second disclosure changes everything: 'The day you buy the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead, in order to perpetuate the name of the dead in his inheritance.' The linking of land redemption to levirate-style marriage obligation is not explicitly prescribed in the Pentateuch — Leviticus 25 covers land redemption; Deuteronomy 25 covers levirate marriage — but the custom of linking them in practice reflects the social reality that land and lineage were inseparable. A redeemed field would pass to the son born of the dead man's widow; the redeemer therefore could not simply acquire the land without the heir obligation that came with it. This complication changes the nearer go'el's calculation entirely.
6: The nearer go'el's refusal — 'I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I damage my own inheritance' — is legally coherent. If he marries Ruth and she bears a son, that son will inherit the redeemed Elimelech field; the nearer go'el would have paid the redemption price but the land would pass to a child who is legally Elimelech's heir, not his own. In practical terms, he would be paying for land that reverts to another family's lineage. His calculation is rational within the framework of the go'el institution — he is protecting his own children's inheritance by declining. His refusal, whatever its motivation, opens the way for Boaz, which is YHWH's providential arrangement working through ordinary human self-interest.
7: The narrator's parenthetical explanation of the sandal custom — 'This was the custom in former times in Israel concerning redeeming and exchanging: to confirm a transaction, the one drew off his sandal and gave it to the other, and this was the manner of attesting in Israel' — is significant because it identifies the custom as archaic even in the narrator's time. The sandal transaction here differs from the Deuteronomy 25:9 levirate-refusal ceremony (where the widow pulls off the shoe of the refusing brother-in-law in public shame). Here the sandal exchange is an agreed transaction between two consenting parties — a symbolic transfer of legal right. The sandal may represent the 'right to walk on the land' — to own and use it — being transferred from one party to another.
8: 'Then the redeemer said to Boaz, Buy it for yourself.' So he drew off his sandal. The legal act is complete: in the presence of ten witnesses and all the gate assembly, the nearer go'el has formally transferred his redemption right to Boaz by handing over his sandal. This is a public, irrevocable, witnessed transaction — it cannot later be contested. The legal formalism of the scene — the gate, the ten elders, the formal speeches, the sandal exchange — reflects the elaborate oral-legal architecture of a society without written contracts. Memory of the gate assembly replaced the written document; the elders' witness was the legal record.
9: Boaz's formal declaration — 'You are witnesses today that I have bought from the hand of Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech and all that belonged to Chilion and to Mahlon' — is a complete legal formula invoking the assembly as witnesses. The triple naming (Elimelech, Chilion, Mahlon) ensures that every possible claim on the property is legally addressed; no unnamed heir or claimant can later arise. The formula 'you are witnesses today' appears in other OT legal contexts (Joshua 24:22; 1 Samuel 12:5) as the standard verbal ratification of a legally binding agreement before witnesses.
10: 'Also Ruth the Moabite, the widow of Mahlon, I have bought to be my wife, to perpetuate the name of the dead in his inheritance, that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his native place. You are witnesses today.' Boaz's declaration accomplishes three things simultaneously: it claims Ruth as his wife, it identifies the purpose (perpetuating Mahlon's name), and it extends the legal witness to both transactions. The phrase 'cut off from his brothers and from the gate of his native place' reflects the ANE horror of familial extinction — dying without descendants who can maintain one's name was considered the worst fate. The go'el and levirate institutions existed specifically to prevent this extinction.
11: The communal blessing — 'May YHWH make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and Leah, who together built up the house of Israel' — invokes the matriarchs of the twelve tribes. Rachel and Leah are paired as the two mothers of all Israel; their cooperative 'building of the house' (through their sons and handmaids) was the origin of the twelve-tribe structure. The blessing for Ruth as a member of this foundational community of matriarchs places her alongside the most honored women of Israel's covenant history. The elders' recognition of Ruth as worthy of this blessing is the community's formal integration of the Moabite woman into the covenant people.
12: The blessing continues: 'and may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, because of the offspring that YHWH will give you by this young woman.' The reference to Perez and Tamar is theologically charged: Tamar was a non-Israelite (probably Canaanite) widow who used an unconventional method (disguise and seduction) to secure her levirate right from Judah, and her twin sons Perez and Zerah became the foundation of the Judahite clan (Gen 38). The parallel to Ruth is exact: another non-Israelite widow securing her levirate right by unconventional means, producing offspring who will become the Davidic line. The elders' blessing invokes the very precedent that validates Ruth's action.
13: The marriage, union, and conception — 'YHWH gave her conception' — invokes divine agency explicitly. Barrenness in the Hebrew Bible is a theological condition as much as a biological one; YHWH opens and closes the womb (Genesis 20:18; Ruth 29:31; Ruth 30:2; 1 Samuel 1:5-6). Ruth's conception is attributed directly to YHWH's opening of her womb, not merely to the marriage. This is consistent with the book's theology throughout: the 'coincidences' that have brought Ruth to Boaz's field, then to his threshing floor, then to his household, are YHWH's providential acts executed through ordinary human hesed. The divine and human are interwoven throughout Ruth; neither operates without the other.
14: The women's blessing of Naomi — 'Blessed be YHWH, who has not left you this day without a redeemer, and may his name be renowned in Israel' — closes the theological arc that Naomi's lament opened in Ruth 1:20-21 ('Do not call me Naomi, call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me... YHWH has brought me back empty'). YHWH has not left Naomi without a go'el; the redeemer the book identified in chapter 2 (Boaz) has acted, and now the child Obed is the redeemer in the next generation. The women's blessing forms the human chorus that interprets YHWH's action — a function similar to the Psalms' commentary on YHWH's deeds in history.
15: 'He shall be to you a restorer of life (mēšîḇ nep̄eš) and a nourisher of your old age, for your daughter-in-law who loves you, who is more to you than seven sons, has given birth to him.' The phrase mēšîḇ nep̄eš (restorer of nepeš, the vital self/life force) is used elsewhere for YHWH's restorative action (Psalms 19:7; Ruth 23:3). The women apply it to both Obed and Ruth: the child will provide for Naomi's old age, and Ruth's hesed toward Naomi is explicitly valued above seven sons — an extraordinary statement in a culture where sons were the highest social value. The seven-son comparison (the ideal family: 1 Samuel 2:5; Job 42:13) being exceeded by one faithful foreign daughter-in-law is the book's final inversion of normal social values.
16: Naomi takes the child and lays him in her bosom and becomes his nurse (ômenek H546 — same root as the word for a male guardian/tutor, here used for a female caretaker). The image of Naomi nursing Obed is powerful in context: the woman who complained of going out full and returning empty (Ruth 1:21) is now full — holding the child who embodies YHWH's reversal of her condition. The act of nursing also functionally adopts Obed as Naomi's son, ensuring her care in old age. The covenant community's recognition that 'a son has been born to Naomi' (v17) means the community officially recognizes this adoptive relationship.
17: The neighbors' naming of the child — 'They gave him a name, saying, “A son has been born to Naomi”' — is an unusual feature: normally the father or mother names the child. The community's act of naming suggests that this child belongs to the whole community and not only to his parents; his birth is a communal event because it is the completion of the book's covenant purpose. Obed (עֹובֵד) means 'servant' or 'worshiper' — the one who serves and worships YHWH. 'He was the father of Jesse, the father of David' — the genealogical climax the book has been building toward. Ruth the Moabite is David's great-grandmother, and through David, in the messianic genealogy, an ancestor of the Christ.
18: The closing genealogy (vv18-22) traces the line from Perez (son of Judah and Tamar, Gen 38) to David — ten generations spanning approximately 400-500 years (from the patriarchal period to the early monarchy). The genealogy echoes the structure of the Gen 5 and Gen 11 genealogies (ten generations from Adam to Noah; ten from Shem to Abraham) — it is stylized to signal a divinely ordered sequence. The genealogy functions as a theological claim: the line from Perez through Boaz to David is YHWH's intentional construction, not historical accident. Each link in the chain — including Tamar's unconventional conception and Ruth's Moabite origin — is part of the divine design.
19: Hezron, Ram, Amminadab — the middle generations of the Perez-to-David chain. These names appear in the tribal genealogies of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:5-15) and in both of the NT Davidic genealogies (Matthew 1:3-4; Luke 3:32-33). Amminadab is the father-in-law of Aaron (Exodus 6:23), placing this generation at the time of the exodus. The genealogy thus provides a chronological framework: Perez (pre-Egyptian sojourn) → Amminadab (exodus generation) → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz (early judges period) → Obed → Jesse → David (ca. 1000 BCE). The time-span compression of the genealogy is consistent with other ANE genealogical conventions, which omit intermediate generations.
20: Nahshon son of Amminadab is the leader of the tribe of Judah in the wilderness (Numbers 1:7; Ruth 2:3; Ruth 7:12); his prominence in the exodus and wilderness period makes him one of the named ancestors of the Davidic line. Salmon is his son, and Matthew 1:5 names Salmon's wife as Rahab — Rahab the Canaanite woman who protected the Israelite spies (Josh 2; Ruth 6:22-25). If the Rahab of Joshua is Boaz's mother, then both Boaz's parents were associated with Gentile women who entered the covenant community through exceptional faith and loyalty — a pattern strikingly parallel to Ruth herself. Matthew's genealogy preserves four women (Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba) as the four maternal exceptions in the otherwise male lineage, all with non-standard entry into Israel's covenant story.
21: Salmon begot Boaz — closing the loop from Rahab's generation to the protagonist of the book. The genealogy economically presents the entire chain without elaboration, trusting that the book has already told the story. The genealogy's function in Ruth is parallel to Matthew 1: it places an individual story within YHWH's long covenant purpose. Ruth's hesed to Naomi, Boaz's hesed to Ruth and Naomi, and YHWH's hesed in arranging their encounter — all of this serves the genealogical purpose of producing Obed, producing Jesse, producing David. The personal story and the cosmic purpose converge in the genealogy.
22: Jesse begot David — the final line, the climactic name. The genealogy's arrival at David is the book's final declaration of purpose. Ruth the Moabite woman, through her covenant loyalty and YHWH's providential arrangement, is the great-grandmother of Israel's greatest king and the ancestor (through David) of the Messiah (Matthew 1:1: 'Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham'). The book of Ruth is thus simultaneously a story about hesed in action, a legal case-study in the go'el institution, and a theological explanation of why a Gentile woman's faithfulness became the vehicle for the Davidic line. The Gentile inclusion in the covenant community is not an afterthought of the NT; it begins, structurally and genealogically, in Ruth.
